ruling_hanafi: Haram - derived from animal collagen, typically pork
ruling_shafii: Haram - derived from animal collagen, typically pork
ruling_maliki: Haram - derived from animal collagen, typically pork
ruling_hanbali: Haram - derived from animal collagen, typically pork
explanation: Gelatin is made from animal bones and skin, most commonly from pigs. Unless certified halal (from halal-slaughtered cattle or fish), it is considered haram.
source_reference: JAKIM Malaysia E-Number Guide; ISA Halal Foundation

ruling_hanafi: Haram - pork fat is categorically prohibited
ruling_shafii: Haram - pork fat is categorically prohibited
ruling_maliki: Haram - pork fat is categorically prohibited
ruling_hanbali: Haram - pork fat is categorically prohibited
explanation: Lard is rendered fat from pigs. It is categorically haram in all schools of Islamic jurisprudence as it is a direct pork product.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - derived from crushed insects, insects are impure
ruling_shafii: Haram - insects are not permissible to consume
ruling_maliki: Permissible with reservation - Maliki school permits consumption of insects in some interpretations
ruling_hanbali: Haram - insects are not permissible to consume
explanation: Carmine is a red dye extracted from crushed cochineal insects. The majority of scholars consider it haram because insects are not halal. The Maliki school has a more lenient view on insect consumption, but most authorities still advise avoidance.
source_reference: JAKIM Malaysia E-Number Guide; Islamic Food and Nutrition Council of America (IFANCA)

ruling_hanafi: Haram - derived from animal bones of unknown slaughter
ruling_shafii: Haram - derived from animal bones of unknown slaughter
ruling_maliki: Haram - derived from animal bones of unknown slaughter
ruling_hanbali: Haram - derived from animal bones of unknown slaughter
explanation: E542 is calcium phosphate derived from animal bones. Since the source animal's slaughter method is typically unknown and may include pork, it is classified as haram.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - direct pork product
explanation: Pork skin is a direct derivative of swine and is categorically haram in all schools of Islamic jurisprudence.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - intoxicants are prohibited
explanation: Ethanol as a beverage alcohol or when present in significant quantities in food is haram. All schools agree that intoxicants are prohibited. Trace amounts that occur naturally or evaporate during cooking may be treated differently.
source_reference: Quran 5:90; JAKIM Malaysia; European Council for Fatwa and Research

ruling_hanafi: Haram - direct pork product
explanation: Bacon is cured pork meat, typically from the belly or back of a pig. It is categorically haram unless explicitly labeled as turkey bacon or beef bacon from halal-slaughtered animals.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - direct pork product
explanation: Ham is processed pork meat from the leg of a pig. It is categorically haram in all schools of Islamic jurisprudence.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - direct pork product
explanation: Prosciutto is Italian dry-cured ham made from pork leg. It is categorically haram.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - direct pork product
explanation: Pancetta is Italian cured pork belly. It is categorically haram as a direct pork product.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - pork-based sausage
explanation: Traditional chorizo is a pork sausage. It is haram unless explicitly made from halal meat (beef or chicken chorizo alternatives exist).

ruling_hanafi: Haram - flowing blood is explicitly prohibited
ruling_shafii: Haram - flowing blood is explicitly prohibited
ruling_maliki: Haram - flowing blood is explicitly prohibited
ruling_hanbali: Haram - flowing blood is explicitly prohibited
explanation: Flowing blood (dam masfuh) is explicitly prohibited in the Quran (6:145). Blood sausages, black pudding, and any food containing blood as an ingredient are haram.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - enzyme typically derived from pig stomach lining
ruling_shafii: Haram - enzyme typically derived from pig stomach lining
ruling_maliki: Haram - enzyme typically derived from pig stomach lining
ruling_hanbali: Haram - enzyme typically derived from pig stomach lining
explanation: Pepsin is a digestive enzyme most commonly extracted from the stomach lining of pigs. Unless specified as microbial or from halal-slaughtered animals, it is considered haram.

ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - some Hanafi scholars permit rennet even from non-halal slaughtered animals as the enzyme undergoes transformation
ruling_shafii: Haram - must be from halal-slaughtered animal
ruling_maliki: Mushbooh - some Maliki scholars apply istihalah principle
ruling_hanbali: Haram - must be from halal-slaughtered animal
explanation: Animal rennet is an enzyme from the stomach lining of calves used in cheese-making. Its ruling varies by madhab due to differing views on whether the enzyme undergoes sufficient transformation (istihalah). When the source animal's slaughter status is unknown, it is best avoided.
source_reference: JAKIM Malaysia; European Council for Fatwa and Research

ruling_hanafi: Haram - unless from halal-slaughtered animal
explanation: Tallow is rendered animal fat, usually from cattle or sheep. When the slaughter status is unknown (as in most commercial products), it is considered haram. Halal-certified tallow from properly slaughtered animals is permissible.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - animal fat of unknown slaughter origin
ruling_shafii: Haram - animal fat of unknown slaughter origin
ruling_maliki: Haram - animal fat of unknown slaughter origin
ruling_hanbali: Haram - animal fat of unknown slaughter origin
explanation: Animal shortening is a solid fat derived from animal sources. Unless certified halal, the source is typically pork or non-halal slaughtered animals. Vegetable shortening is a halal alternative.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - secretion of lac insect, insects are impure
ruling_maliki: Mushbooh - Maliki school has more lenient view on insect products
explanation: Shellac is a resin secreted by the female lac bug. It is used as a coating on candies, pills, and fruits. Most scholars consider it haram as it is an insect secretion, though some Maliki scholars may permit it.
source_reference: JAKIM Malaysia E-Number Guide; MUIS Singapore

ruling_hanafi: Haram - secretion from civet cat glands
explanation: Civet is a secretion from the perineal glands of the civet cat. It was historically used as a flavoring (e.g., in some coffees) and in perfumery. It is considered haram when consumed.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - enzyme from animal source of unknown slaughter
ruling_shafii: Haram - enzyme from animal source of unknown slaughter
ruling_maliki: Haram - enzyme from animal source of unknown slaughter
ruling_hanbali: Haram - enzyme from animal source of unknown slaughter
explanation: Lipase is a fat-splitting enzyme used in cheese and other dairy products. When derived from animal sources (especially pork), it is haram. Microbial lipase is a halal alternative.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - pork-derived protein
explanation: Collagen derived from pork skin and bones is haram. It is commonly found in supplements, cosmetics, and food products. Marine or bovine halal-certified collagen are permissible alternatives.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - derived from cochineal insects
ruling_maliki: Mushbooh - Maliki school has more lenient view on insect-derived substances
explanation: Carminic acid is the coloring compound extracted from cochineal insects, used to produce carmine dye (E120). The same rulings apply as for cochineal/carmine.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - alcoholic beverage is prohibited
explanation: Wine and all alcoholic beverages are explicitly prohibited in the Quran. Using wine in cooking is also haram as the alcohol may not fully evaporate and the substance itself is impure (najis) according to the majority of scholars.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - alcoholic beverage
explanation: Beer is an alcoholic beverage made from fermented grains. It is haram in all schools. Non-alcoholic beer (0.0% ABV) is generally considered halal.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - distilled alcoholic spirit
explanation: Rum is a distilled alcoholic spirit made from sugarcane. Its use in food and beverages is haram. Artificial rum flavoring without alcohol content may be permissible.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - fermented rice wine containing alcohol
ruling_shafii: Haram - fermented rice wine containing alcohol
ruling_maliki: Haram - fermented rice wine containing alcohol
ruling_hanbali: Haram - fermented rice wine containing alcohol
explanation: Mirin is a Japanese rice wine with approximately 14% alcohol content. It is haram. Halal mirin substitutes (mirin-style seasonings without alcohol) are available.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - alcoholic beverage
explanation: Sake is a Japanese alcoholic beverage made from fermented rice. It contains approximately 15-20% alcohol and is haram.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - distilled alcoholic spirit
explanation: Brandy is a distilled spirit made from wine or fermented fruit juice. It is haram as an intoxicating substance.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - alcoholic beverage
explanation: Liqueurs are sweetened alcoholic spirits. They are haram regardless of their flavoring or sugar content, as they contain significant alcohol.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - pork-derived enzyme
explanation: Enzymes derived from pork (including pepsin, lipase, and trypsin from pork sources) are haram. They are used in cheese, bread, and other processed foods.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - derived from animal bones
explanation: When derived from animal bones of unknown origin, this additive is haram. Plant-derived alternatives exist.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - when derived from non-halal animal fat
ruling_shafii: Haram - when derived from non-halal animal fat
ruling_maliki: Haram - when derived from non-halal animal fat
ruling_hanbali: Haram - when derived from non-halal animal fat
explanation: Stearic acid derived from animal fats (particularly pork) is haram. The source is often undisclosed in ingredient lists, making it a concern. Plant-derived stearic acid is halal.
source_reference: JAKIM Malaysia E-Number Guide; ISA Halal Foundation

ruling_hanafi: Haram - often derived from human hair or pig bristles
ruling_shafii: Haram - often derived from human hair or pig bristles
ruling_maliki: Haram - often derived from human hair or pig bristles
ruling_hanbali: Haram - often derived from human hair or pig bristles
explanation: L-cystine (E921) is often derived from human hair, pig bristles, or poultry feathers. When from human or pork sources, it is haram. Synthetic or plant-derived versions exist but are less common.
source_reference: JAKIM Malaysia E-Number Guide; MUIS Singapore

ruling_hanafi: Halal - musk is considered pure (tahir) in Hanafi school
ruling_shafii: Halal - musk is considered pure (tahir) in Shafi'i school
ruling_maliki: Halal - musk is considered pure (tahir) in Maliki school
ruling_hanbali: Halal - musk is considered pure (tahir) in Hanbali school
explanation: Natural musk from the musk deer is actually considered pure and halal by most scholars based on hadith. However, obtaining it may involve harming the animal. The Prophet (PBUH) described musk favorably. When synthetically produced, there is no issue. Listed as haram here due to animal welfare concerns in modern sourcing, though the substance itself is traditionally considered tahir.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - secretion from beaver glands
explanation: Castoreum is a secretion from the castor glands of beavers, sometimes used as a natural flavoring. It is considered haram as it comes from an animal not slaughtered according to Islamic law.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - typically derived from non-halal animal muscle tissue
ruling_shafii: Haram - typically derived from non-halal animal muscle tissue
ruling_maliki: Haram - typically derived from non-halal animal muscle tissue
ruling_hanbali: Haram - typically derived from non-halal animal muscle tissue
explanation: Carnosine is a dipeptide found in animal muscle tissue. Commercial sources are typically from non-halal slaughtered animals, making it haram unless certified otherwise.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - derived from animal hooves, horns, or hair of unknown origin
ruling_shafii: Haram - derived from animal parts of unknown slaughter
ruling_maliki: Haram - derived from animal parts of unknown slaughter
ruling_hanbali: Haram - derived from animal parts of unknown slaughter
explanation: Keratin used in food supplements is typically derived from animal hooves, horns, feathers, or hair. Without halal certification, the slaughter status is unknown and it is considered haram.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - when derived from non-halal animal sources
ruling_shafii: Haram - when derived from non-halal animal sources
ruling_maliki: Haram - when derived from non-halal animal sources
ruling_hanbali: Haram - when derived from non-halal animal sources
explanation: While lactic acid is mostly produced by bacterial fermentation (halal), some sources use animal-derived substrates. When confirmed animal-derived, it is haram unless from halal sources.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - when stearic acid is from non-halal animal fat
ruling_shafii: Haram - when stearic acid is from non-halal animal fat
ruling_maliki: Haram - when stearic acid is from non-halal animal fat
ruling_hanbali: Haram - when stearic acid is from non-halal animal fat
explanation: When the stearic acid component is derived from animal fat (especially pork), this emulsifier is haram. Plant-derived versions are halal.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - direct pork product
explanation: Natural sausage casings are often made from pig intestines. They are categorically haram. Halal alternatives include collagen casings, cellulose casings, or casings from halal-slaughtered animals.

ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - halal if plant-derived, haram if from non-halal animal fat
ruling_shafii: Mushbooh - halal if plant-derived, haram if from non-halal animal fat
ruling_maliki: Mushbooh - halal if plant-derived, haram if from non-halal animal fat
ruling_hanbali: Mushbooh - halal if plant-derived, haram if from non-halal animal fat
explanation: Glycerol can be derived from plant oils (halal) or animal fats (potentially haram). Without clear labeling of the source, it is considered mushbooh (doubtful). Most commercial glycerol today is plant-derived, but verification is recommended.
source_reference: JAKIM Malaysia E-Number Guide; MUIS Singapore

ingredient_name: Mono and Diglycerides of Fatty Acids
ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - source of fatty acids is often undisclosed, could be animal or plant
ruling_shafii: Mushbooh - source of fatty acids is often undisclosed, could be animal or plant
ruling_maliki: Mushbooh - source of fatty acids is often undisclosed, could be animal or plant
ruling_hanbali: Mushbooh - source of fatty acids is often undisclosed, could be animal or plant
explanation: E471 is one of the most common mushbooh ingredients. These emulsifiers can be derived from plant oils (soy, palm) or animal fats (pork, beef). Manufacturers rarely disclose the source, making it doubtful without halal certification.
source_reference: JAKIM Malaysia E-Number Guide; MUIS Singapore; IFANCA

ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - could contain animal-derived or alcohol-based ingredients
ruling_shafii: Mushbooh - could contain animal-derived or alcohol-based ingredients
ruling_maliki: Mushbooh - could contain animal-derived or alcohol-based ingredients
ruling_hanbali: Mushbooh - could contain animal-derived or alcohol-based ingredients
explanation: Natural flavors is a catch-all term that can include extracts from plants, animals, or fermentation. They may contain alcohol as a carrier or animal-derived components like castoreum. Without specific disclosure, they are mushbooh.

ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - depends on the rennet used in cheese production; if animal rennet was used, whey may be affected
ruling_shafii: Mushbooh - depends on the rennet used in cheese production
ruling_maliki: Mushbooh - depends on the rennet used in cheese production
ruling_hanbali: Mushbooh - depends on the rennet used in cheese production
explanation: Whey is a byproduct of cheese-making. Its halal status depends on whether halal-compliant rennet and enzymes were used. If animal rennet (from non-halal source) was used, the whey is affected.

ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - source is often undisclosed; could be microbial (halal), plant (halal), or animal (potentially haram)
explanation: Enzymes used in food processing can come from microbial, plant, or animal sources. Without clear labeling, the source is unknown. Microbial and plant enzymes are halal; animal enzymes require halal certification.

ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - commonly derived from human hair, duck feathers, or pig bristles; synthetic version is halal
ruling_shafii: Mushbooh - commonly derived from human hair, duck feathers, or pig bristles
ruling_maliki: Mushbooh - commonly derived from human hair, duck feathers, or pig bristles
ruling_hanbali: Mushbooh - commonly derived from human hair, duck feathers, or pig bristles
explanation: L-Cysteine is an amino acid used as a dough conditioner in bread. It can be derived from human hair (haram), pig bristles (haram), duck feathers (halal if properly sourced), or produced synthetically (halal). The source is rarely disclosed.
source_reference: JAKIM Malaysia E-Number Guide; IFANCA; MUIS Singapore

ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - soy and sunflower lecithin are halal; egg lecithin is halal; animal-fat derived lecithin is haram
explanation: Lecithin is commonly derived from soy (halal), sunflower (halal), or eggs (halal). However, it can also be derived from animal fat. When labeled simply as 'lecithin' without specifying the source, it is mushbooh.
source_reference: JAKIM Malaysia E-Number Guide; MUIS Singapore

ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - contains 35%+ alcohol as extraction solvent; small amounts in food may be permissible if no intoxication occurs
ruling_maliki: Mushbooh - small amounts used in cooking where alcohol evaporates may be permissible
explanation: Pure vanilla extract contains at least 35% alcohol by FDA standards. The rulings vary: some scholars permit it when used in small quantities in cooking (alcohol evaporates), while others prohibit it due to its alcohol content. Vanilla powder or vanilla bean paste are safer alternatives.
source_reference: JAKIM Malaysia; European Council for Fatwa and Research; IFANCA

ruling_hanafi: Halal - wine that naturally transforms into vinegar is halal through istihalah (complete transformation)
ruling_shafii: Haram - deliberately making vinegar from wine is not permitted; only naturally occurring transformation is accepted
ruling_maliki: Halal - vinegar from wine is permissible as the transformation is complete
ruling_hanbali: Haram - deliberately making vinegar from wine is prohibited; vinegar must come from halal sources
explanation: Wine vinegar is a key area of madhab disagreement. The Hanafi and Maliki schools apply the istihalah principle, holding that wine fully transformed into vinegar becomes a new, halal substance. The Shafi'i and Hanbali schools distinguish between natural and deliberate transformation, generally prohibiting vinegar deliberately made from wine.
source_reference: JAKIM Malaysia; European Council for Fatwa and Research; Al-Qaradawi's 'The Lawful and the Prohibited in Islam'

ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - can be from plant (halal) or animal (potentially haram) sources
ruling_shafii: Mushbooh - can be from plant or animal sources
ruling_maliki: Mushbooh - can be from plant or animal sources
ruling_hanbali: Mushbooh - can be from plant or animal sources
explanation: Stearic acid is a fatty acid that can be derived from plant fats (palm, coconut) or animal fats (tallow, lard). Without source specification, it is mushbooh.
source_reference: JAKIM Malaysia E-Number Guide; ISA Halal Foundation

ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - stearic acid component may be plant or animal derived
ruling_shafii: Mushbooh - stearic acid component may be plant or animal derived
ruling_maliki: Mushbooh - stearic acid component may be plant or animal derived
ruling_hanbali: Mushbooh - stearic acid component may be plant or animal derived
explanation: Magnesium stearate is widely used as a tablet lubricant in supplements and medicines. The stearic acid component can come from plant or animal sources, making it mushbooh without verification.

ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - stearic acid component may be plant or animal derived
ruling_shafii: Mushbooh - stearic acid component may be plant or animal derived
ruling_maliki: Mushbooh - stearic acid component may be plant or animal derived
ruling_hanbali: Mushbooh - stearic acid component may be plant or animal derived
explanation: Like magnesium stearate, the stearic acid in calcium stearate can be from plant or animal sources. Mushbooh unless the source is verified.

source: synthetic (from plant or animal fatty acids)
ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - fatty acid source may be plant or animal
ruling_shafii: Mushbooh - fatty acid source may be plant or animal
ruling_maliki: Mushbooh - fatty acid source may be plant or animal
ruling_hanbali: Mushbooh - fatty acid source may be plant or animal
explanation: Polysorbate 60 is an emulsifier made from sorbitol and stearic acid. The stearic acid component can be from plant or animal sources, making it mushbooh.

source: synthetic (from plant or animal fatty acids)
ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - oleic acid source may be plant or animal
ruling_shafii: Mushbooh - oleic acid source may be plant or animal
ruling_maliki: Mushbooh - oleic acid source may be plant or animal
ruling_hanbali: Mushbooh - oleic acid source may be plant or animal
explanation: Polysorbate 80 is an emulsifier derived from sorbitol and oleic acid. The oleic acid can come from plant or animal fat, making it mushbooh without source verification.

source: synthetic (from plant or animal fatty acids)
ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - stearic acid component may be from plant or animal sources
ruling_shafii: Mushbooh - stearic acid component may be from plant or animal sources
ruling_maliki: Mushbooh - stearic acid component may be from plant or animal sources
ruling_hanbali: Mushbooh - stearic acid component may be from plant or animal sources
explanation: SSL is a common bread emulsifier. The stearic acid component can be from plant oils or animal fats. Without halal certification, the source is unknown.

ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - glycerol and stearic acid sources may be plant or animal
ruling_shafii: Mushbooh - glycerol and stearic acid sources may be plant or animal
ruling_maliki: Mushbooh - glycerol and stearic acid sources may be plant or animal
ruling_hanbali: Mushbooh - glycerol and stearic acid sources may be plant or animal
explanation: GMS is a common food emulsifier. Both the glycerol and stearic acid components can be from plant or animal sources, making it mushbooh.

ingredient_name: Diacetyl Tartaric Acid Esters of Mono and Diglycerides
ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - fatty acid source may be plant or animal
ruling_shafii: Mushbooh - fatty acid source may be plant or animal
ruling_maliki: Mushbooh - fatty acid source may be plant or animal
ruling_hanbali: Mushbooh - fatty acid source may be plant or animal
explanation: DATEM is a dough conditioner commonly used in bread. The mono and diglyceride component can be from plant or animal fat sources.

source: synthetic (from plant or animal fatty acids)
ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - stearic acid may be from plant or animal sources
ruling_shafii: Mushbooh - stearic acid may be from plant or animal sources
ruling_maliki: Mushbooh - stearic acid may be from plant or animal sources
ruling_hanbali: Mushbooh - stearic acid may be from plant or animal sources
explanation: This emulsifier contains stearic acid that may be from plant or animal sources. Without source specification, it is mushbooh.

ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - fatty acid source may be plant or animal
ruling_shafii: Mushbooh - fatty acid source may be plant or animal
ruling_maliki: Mushbooh - fatty acid source may be plant or animal
ruling_hanbali: Mushbooh - fatty acid source may be plant or animal
explanation: These emulsifiers are esters of sucrose with fatty acids. The fatty acid component can be from plant or animal sources.

ingredient_name: Propylene Glycol Esters of Fatty Acids
source: synthetic (from plant or animal fatty acids)
ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - fatty acid source may be plant or animal
ruling_shafii: Mushbooh - fatty acid source may be plant or animal
ruling_maliki: Mushbooh - fatty acid source may be plant or animal
ruling_hanbali: Mushbooh - fatty acid source may be plant or animal
explanation: The fatty acid component of this emulsifier can come from plant or animal sources, making it mushbooh without verification.

ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - depends on source; microbial and plant rennet are halal; animal rennet is disputed
ruling_shafii: Mushbooh - animal rennet from non-halal slaughtered animal is haram; microbial is halal
ruling_hanbali: Mushbooh - animal rennet from non-halal slaughtered animal is haram; microbial is halal
explanation: Rennet is an enzyme used in cheese-making. Its status depends entirely on the source: microbial (halal), plant-derived (halal), or animal-derived (mushbooh to haram depending on madhab and slaughter status).
source_reference: JAKIM Malaysia; European Council for Fatwa and Research

ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - microbial origin is halal but growth medium may include animal-derived nutrients
explanation: Gellan gum is produced by bacterial fermentation, which is inherently halal. However, the growth medium for the bacteria may contain animal-derived nutrients, making verification advisable.

source: animal (lanolin from sheep wool) or plant (lichen)
ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - commonly derived from lanolin (sheep wool grease); plant-based (lichen) versions are halal
ruling_shafii: Mushbooh - depends on source and processing
ruling_maliki: Mushbooh - depends on source and processing
ruling_hanbali: Mushbooh - depends on source and processing
explanation: Most vitamin D3 is derived from lanolin extracted from sheep wool. While wool is not haram, the processing may involve non-halal substances. Plant-derived D3 from lichen is a halal alternative. D2 (ergocalciferol) from mushrooms is also halal.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - insect-derived shellac coating
ruling_maliki: Mushbooh - Maliki school more lenient on insect products
explanation: Confectioner's glaze is essentially shellac (E904), a resin from lac insects. Used to coat candies, chocolates, and pills. Most scholars consider it haram, though there is some difference of opinion.
source_reference: JAKIM Malaysia E-Number Guide; MUIS Singapore

ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - can be from plant oils (halal) or animal fats (potentially haram)
explanation: Oleic acid is a fatty acid found in both plant oils and animal fats. Without clear source labeling, it is mushbooh.

ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - found in both palm oil (halal) and animal fats
explanation: Palmitic acid is a saturated fatty acid found abundantly in palm oil (halal) but also in animal fats. Source verification is needed.

ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - fish oil is halal, but capsule (gelatin) may be haram; algae-based is halal
ruling_shafii: Mushbooh - fish source is halal but encapsulation may use non-halal gelatin
explanation: Omega-3 from fish or algae is halal in itself, but supplements often use gelatin capsules that may be pork-derived. The oil source and encapsulation material both need verification.

ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - some Hanafi scholars apply istihalah (transformation) principle, making it halal; others disagree
ruling_shafii: Haram - derived from animal bones of unknown slaughter
ruling_hanbali: Haram - derived from animal bones of unknown slaughter
explanation: Bone char is used in sugar refining as a decolorizing filter. It is made from cattle bones. While the sugar does not contain bone char, it was processed using it. Scholars differ on whether the transformation principle applies.

ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - milk protein is halal, but processing may involve non-halal enzymes
ruling_shafii: Mushbooh - processing enzymes may be animal-derived
ruling_maliki: Mushbooh - processing enzymes may be animal-derived
ruling_hanbali: Mushbooh - processing enzymes may be animal-derived
explanation: Casein is a milk protein that is inherently halal. However, the processing may involve animal-derived enzymes or acids, making it mushbooh without verification of production methods.

ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - could be plant-based (halal) or animal-derived (potentially haram)
explanation: When a product lists 'gelling agent' or 'thickening agent' without specifying the source, it could be anything from halal pectin/agar to haram gelatin. Verification is needed.

ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - could be plant-based (halal) or animal-derived (potentially haram)
explanation: Unspecified emulsifiers could be plant-derived (soy lecithin, etc.) or animal-derived (mono/diglycerides from animal fat). Without clear identification, they are mushbooh.

ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - lauric acid can be from coconut oil (halal) or animal fat
explanation: SLS is commonly derived from coconut or palm oil (halal), but can also be from animal fat. Used in toothpaste and some food products.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - beeswax is considered pure as honey is halal and beeswax is a bee product
explanation: Beeswax is generally considered halal by most scholars as it is a product of bees, similar to honey which is explicitly mentioned as beneficial in the Quran. Listed as mushbooh because some scholars have concerns about insect-derived products, but the majority ruling is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - bee product, similar to honey
explanation: Propolis is a resinous substance collected by bees. Like honey and beeswax, it is generally considered halal. However, propolis extracts may use alcohol as a solvent, which could make them mushbooh.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - bee product
explanation: Royal jelly is a secretion from worker bees. It is generally halal as a bee product. Supplements may contain non-halal capsule materials (gelatin), making them mushbooh.

ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - small amounts of alcohol as a carrier that evaporate during processing may be permissible; some scholars allow if final product has negligible alcohol
ruling_shafii: Haram - any intentional use of alcohol is prohibited regardless of amount
ruling_maliki: Mushbooh - trace amounts may be overlooked if the final product is not intoxicating
ruling_hanbali: Haram - any amount of alcohol is prohibited
explanation: The use of alcohol as a carrier in flavorings is a major area of scholarly disagreement. Hanafi scholars tend to be more lenient when the alcohol evaporates and the final product is not intoxicating. Shafi'i and Hanbali scholars generally prohibit any intentional use. The amount and whether it remains in the final product are key factors.
source_reference: European Council for Fatwa and Research; JAKIM Malaysia; MUIS Singapore

ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - naturally brewed soy sauce contains trace alcohol from fermentation (1-2%); synthetic soy sauce is halal
ruling_shafii: Mushbooh - trace alcohol content is a concern
ruling_maliki: Mushbooh - trace alcohol content is a concern
ruling_hanbali: Mushbooh - trace alcohol content is a concern
explanation: Naturally brewed soy sauce undergoes fermentation that produces small amounts of alcohol (typically 1-2%). Chemically produced (non-brewed) soy sauce does not contain alcohol. Halal-certified soy sauce is available.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - Hanafi school only permits fish with scales from aquatic creatures; shrimp/prawns are makruh tahrim (prohibitively disliked) according to the majority Hanafi view
ruling_shafii: Halal - all seafood is permissible in the Shafi'i school
ruling_maliki: Halal - all seafood is permissible in the Maliki school
ruling_hanbali: Halal - all seafood is permissible in the Hanbali school
explanation: Shrimp is one of the most significant madhab-difference ingredients. The Hanafi school restricts permissible seafood to fish (samak) only, while the other three schools consider all sea creatures halal based on the hadith 'Its water is purifying and its dead are halal.'
source_reference: Sunan Abu Dawud; JAKIM Malaysia; Hanafi fiqh texts (Al-Hidayah)

ruling_hanafi: Haram - not classified as fish (samak) in Hanafi jurisprudence
explanation: Crab is haram in the Hanafi school as it is not a fish. The other three schools permit all sea creatures. This is a well-known area of scholarly disagreement.
source_reference: JAKIM Malaysia; Hanafi fiqh texts; Reliance of the Traveller (Shafi'i)

ruling_hanafi: Haram - not classified as fish (samak) in Hanafi jurisprudence
explanation: Like crab and shrimp, lobster is not permitted in the Hanafi school but is halal in the other three schools.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - not classified as fish (samak) in Hanafi jurisprudence
explanation: Squid and cuttlefish are not fish, so the Hanafi school does not permit them. The Shafi'i, Maliki, and Hanbali schools consider them halal.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - not classified as fish (samak)
explanation: Octopus follows the same ruling pattern as other non-fish sea creatures: haram in Hanafi, halal in the other three schools.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - shellfish are not permitted in Hanafi school
explanation: Mussels are shellfish and follow the Hanafi restriction on non-fish sea creatures.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - shellfish are not permitted in Hanafi school
explanation: Oysters are shellfish. Oyster sauce, while named after oysters, should be checked for actual oyster content. The ruling follows the shellfish pattern across madhabs.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - shellfish are not permitted in Hanafi school
explanation: Clams follow the same ruling as other shellfish: not permitted in the Hanafi school, halal in the others.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - shellfish are not permitted in Hanafi school
explanation: Scallops are bivalve shellfish with the same madhab differences as other shellfish.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - locusts are specifically permitted based on hadith
ruling_shafii: Halal - locusts are specifically permitted based on hadith
ruling_maliki: Halal - locusts and most insects are permissible in Maliki school
ruling_hanbali: Halal - locusts are specifically permitted based on hadith
explanation: Locusts are the one insect unanimously considered halal based on the hadith narrated by Ibn Umar: 'Two dead animals and two types of blood have been made lawful for us: the two dead animals are fish and locusts.' Other insects (crickets, mealworms) are disputed except in the Maliki school which is more permissive.
source_reference: Sunan Ibn Majah; Musnad Ahmad; JAKIM Malaysia

ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - may contain animal-derived fats
explanation: Cocoa butter equivalents are fats used to replace cocoa butter in chocolate. They can be plant-derived (shea, palm) or may contain animal fats. Verification is recommended.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - derived from seaweed, a plant source
explanation: Carrageenan is derived from red seaweed and is inherently halal. Listed as mushbooh because some production processes may use alcohol or animal-derived processing aids, though this is uncommon. Most carrageenan is halal.

source: plant (but may use animal-derived processing aids)
ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - the modification process may involve animal-derived chemicals
ruling_shafii: Mushbooh - processing aids may be animal-derived
ruling_maliki: Mushbooh - processing aids may be animal-derived
ruling_hanbali: Mushbooh - processing aids may be animal-derived
explanation: While the starch base is plant-derived (corn, potato, tapioca), the chemical modification process may use animal-derived acids or enzymes. Most modified starches are halal, but verification is advised.

ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - could be porcine (haram) or microbial (halal)
explanation: When the source of pepsin is not specified, it could be from pork (haram) or microbial fermentation (halal). Most commercial pepsin is porcine, making caution advisable.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - pure and permissible
explanation: Water is inherently pure and halal. It is one of the fundamental necessities of life explicitly mentioned in the Quran as a blessing.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived sweetener
explanation: Sugar derived from sugarcane or sugar beets is halal. Note: some refined white sugar may be processed using bone char as a decolorizer, which is a separate concern for some scholars.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - natural mineral
explanation: Salt is a natural mineral and is categorically halal in all forms (sea salt, rock salt, table salt, etc.).

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived grain product
explanation: Wheat flour in its pure form is halal. However, some breads made with flour may contain L-cysteine (E920) or other additives that require checking.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived grain
explanation: Rice is a staple grain that is categorically halal in all its forms.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived starch
explanation: Corn starch is derived from corn kernels and is halal in its pure form.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - inorganic chemical compound
explanation: Baking soda (sodium bicarbonate) is a purely inorganic compound and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - mixture of baking soda, acid salt, and starch
explanation: Baking powder is a mixture of sodium bicarbonate, an acid salt (cream of tartar or sodium aluminum sulfate), and corn starch. All components are halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - produced by microbial fermentation of sugars
explanation: Citric acid is produced commercially by fungal fermentation of sugars. It is halal and one of the most commonly used food acids.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - synthetically produced or derived from plants
explanation: Ascorbic acid (vitamin C) is produced synthetically or extracted from plant sources. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - extracted from fruit peels (apple, citrus)
explanation: Pectin is a natural gelling agent extracted from fruit peels, primarily apple and citrus. It is a halal alternative to gelatin.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - derived from red algae/seaweed
explanation: Agar-agar is a gelatinous substance derived from red algae (seaweed). It is a popular halal alternative to gelatin, widely used in Asian and halal cuisine.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - derived from guar beans
explanation: Guar gum is a natural thickener derived from the guar bean. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - produced by bacterial fermentation
explanation: Xanthan gum is produced by bacterial fermentation of sugars. It is halal and commonly used as a thickener and stabilizer.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - the acid component of vinegar, produced by bacterial fermentation
explanation: Acetic acid is the main component of vinegar. When produced by bacterial fermentation of ethanol (non-wine based) or synthetically, it is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - naturally found in grapes, produced as a byproduct of winemaking but is a separate substance
ruling_shafii: Halal - the acid itself is a distinct compound from wine
ruling_maliki: Halal - the acid itself is a distinct compound
ruling_hanbali: Halal - the acid itself is a distinct compound
explanation: Tartaric acid naturally occurs in grapes and other fruits. While it can be a byproduct of winemaking, the acid itself is a distinct chemical compound that is not intoxicating and is considered halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - naturally found in apples and other fruits
explanation: Malic acid is found naturally in apples and other fruits. Commercial malic acid is produced synthetically. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - synthetically produced preservative
explanation: Sorbic acid and its salts (potassium sorbate) are synthetic preservatives. They are halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - synthetic preservative or naturally found in berries
explanation: Benzoic acid and sodium benzoate are common preservatives. They are synthetically produced or naturally derived from berries. They are halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived spice
explanation: Turmeric is a root spice used extensively in cooking. It is halal. Curcumin (E100) is the yellow pigment extracted from turmeric.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived spice
explanation: Cumin is a plant-derived spice and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived herb/spice
explanation: Coriander (both leaves and seeds) is a plant-derived herb/spice and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived spice
explanation: Black pepper is a plant-derived spice and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived spice from tree bark
explanation: Cinnamon is a plant-derived spice from tree bark and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived spice/root
explanation: Ginger is a plant root used as a spice and is halal. It is mentioned in the Quran (76:17) as a drink flavoring in paradise.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived
explanation: Garlic is a plant-derived ingredient and is halal. It is makruh (disliked) to eat raw garlic before prayer due to its strong odor, but it is not haram.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived
explanation: Onion is a plant-derived ingredient and is halal. Like garlic, it is makruh to eat raw before prayer due to its odor.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - derived from capsicum peppers
explanation: Paprika is made from dried and ground capsicum peppers. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived spice
explanation: Chili peppers and their derivatives are plant-based and halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived spice
explanation: Cardamom is a plant-derived spice and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived spice from flower buds
explanation: Cloves are dried flower buds and are halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - permissible in small amounts as a spice; large quantities that cause intoxication are haram
ruling_shafii: Halal - permissible as a spice in normal culinary amounts
ruling_maliki: Halal - permissible as a spice in normal culinary amounts
ruling_hanbali: Halal - permissible as a spice in normal culinary amounts
explanation: Nutmeg in small culinary amounts is halal. Very large quantities can have psychoactive effects, which would make excessive consumption haram. Normal use as a spice is permissible.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - blessed oil mentioned in the Quran
explanation: Olive oil is halal and considered blessed. The olive tree is mentioned multiple times in the Quran (6:99, 6:141, 16:11, 24:35).

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived oil
explanation: Sunflower oil is extracted from sunflower seeds and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived oil
explanation: Canola oil is derived from rapeseed plants and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived oil from oil palm fruit
explanation: Palm oil is derived from the fruit of oil palm trees and is halal. It is widely used in the halal food industry.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived oil from coconut
explanation: Coconut oil is extracted from coconut flesh and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived oil
explanation: Soybean oil is extracted from soybeans and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived oil from sesame seeds
explanation: Sesame oil is derived from sesame seeds and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - vinegar is praised in hadith; non-wine vinegar is unanimously halal
explanation: Vinegar made from non-wine sources (apple cider, rice, malt, distilled) is halal in all schools. The Prophet (PBUH) said: 'What a good condiment vinegar is' (Sahih Muslim).

ruling_hanafi: Halal - milk from halal animals is pure and permissible
ruling_shafii: Halal - milk from halal animals is pure
ruling_maliki: Halal - milk from halal animals is pure
ruling_hanbali: Halal - milk from halal animals is pure
explanation: Milk from cows, goats, sheep, and camels is halal. The Quran (16:66) describes milk as pure and palatable. Milk from non-halal animals (e.g., donkey, pig) would not be permissible.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - eggs from halal birds are permissible
ruling_shafii: Halal - eggs from halal birds are permissible
ruling_maliki: Halal - eggs from halal birds are permissible
ruling_hanbali: Halal - eggs from halal birds are permissible
explanation: Eggs from halal birds (chickens, ducks, quails, etc.) are halal. Eggs from non-halal birds are not permissible.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - explicitly mentioned in the Quran as healing
ruling_shafii: Halal - explicitly mentioned in the Quran
ruling_maliki: Halal - explicitly mentioned in the Quran
ruling_hanbali: Halal - explicitly mentioned in the Quran
explanation: Honey is explicitly mentioned in the Quran (16:69) as a healing drink produced by bees. It is halal by consensus of all scholars.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - derived from cacao beans
explanation: Cocoa in its pure form (powder, butter, nibs) is halal. However, chocolate products may contain non-halal additives (gelatin, E471, alcohol, etc.) that must be checked separately.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - the bean/pod itself is a plant product
explanation: The vanilla bean itself is halal. It is the extraction process using alcohol that makes vanilla extract mushbooh. Vanilla powder and vanilla bean paste (without alcohol) are halal alternatives.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - derived from soybeans
explanation: Soy protein in all its forms is halal as it is derived entirely from soybeans.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - made from soybeans and coagulant
explanation: Tofu is made from soybeans and a coagulant (typically calcium sulfate or nigari). It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - derived from potatoes
explanation: Potato starch is derived from potatoes and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - derived from cassava root
explanation: Tapioca starch is derived from cassava root and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived grain
explanation: Oats are a halal grain. Oat-based products should be checked for other added ingredients.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived grain mentioned in hadith
explanation: Barley is a halal grain. The Prophet (PBUH) recommended barley water (talbinah) for healing. Note: barley malt extract may undergo fermentation in some products.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived legume
explanation: Lentils are plant-derived legumes and are halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived legume
explanation: Chickpeas are plant-derived legumes and are halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - tree nut
explanation: Almonds are tree nuts and are halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - tree nut
explanation: Walnuts are tree nuts and are halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - tree nut
explanation: Cashews are tree nuts and are halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - legume
explanation: Peanuts (technically a legume, not a nut) are halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - blessed fruit mentioned in the Quran and Sunnah
explanation: Dates are one of the most significant fruits in Islam, mentioned extensively in the Quran and Sunnah. The Prophet (PBUH) recommended breaking fast with dates.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - fruit mentioned in the Quran
explanation: Figs are mentioned in the Quran (Surah At-Tin, 95:1). They are halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - fruit mentioned in the Quran
explanation: Grapes and their dried forms (raisins, sultanas) are halal. Grapes are mentioned in the Quran (6:99). However, fermented grape products (wine) are haram.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - fruit mentioned in the Quran
explanation: Pomegranate is mentioned in the Quran (6:99, 6:141) as a fruit of paradise. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived fruit
explanation: Bananas are a plant-derived fruit and are halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived fruit
explanation: Apples are a plant-derived fruit and are halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived fruit
explanation: Oranges are a plant-derived fruit and are halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived fruit
explanation: Lemons are a plant-derived fruit and are halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived fruit/vegetable
explanation: Tomatoes and tomato products (paste, sauce, ketchup) are halal in their basic form. Check processed products for additional non-halal ingredients.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived vegetable
explanation: Carrots are a plant-derived vegetable and are halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived vegetable
explanation: Spinach is a plant-derived vegetable and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - derived from peas
explanation: Pea protein is derived from yellow split peas and is halal. It is commonly used as a plant-based protein alternative.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - derived from coconut
explanation: Coconut milk and cream are derived from coconut flesh and are halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - blessed seed mentioned in hadith as a cure for all ailments except death
explanation: Black seed (Nigella sativa) is explicitly mentioned in hadith: 'In the black seed there is healing for every disease except death' (Sahih Bukhari). It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived spice from crocus flower stigmas
explanation: Saffron is derived from the stigmas of the crocus flower and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived herb
explanation: Thyme is a plant-derived herb and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived herb
explanation: Oregano is a plant-derived herb and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived herb
explanation: Basil is a plant-derived herb and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived herb and antioxidant
explanation: Rosemary and rosemary extract (E392, used as an antioxidant) are plant-derived and halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived herb
explanation: Mint is a plant-derived herb and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived herb
explanation: Parsley is a plant-derived herb and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived spice
explanation: Fennel is a plant-derived spice and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived spice from tree fruit
explanation: Star anise is a spice from the fruit of an evergreen tree and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived herb
explanation: Lemongrass is a plant-derived herb and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived root/spice
explanation: Galangal is a root related to ginger and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - derived from annatto seeds
explanation: Annatto is a natural orange-red food colorant derived from the seeds of the achiote tree. It is halal and a common alternative to artificial colors.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - naturally found in carrots and other vegetables; also produced synthetically
explanation: Beta-carotene is a natural pigment found in carrots, sweet potatoes, and other orange vegetables. It is halal whether natural or synthetic.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - natural green pigment from plants
explanation: Chlorophyll is the green pigment found in plants. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - when derived from plant sources (coconut shell, wood)
explanation: Activated charcoal from plant sources (coconut shell, bamboo, wood) is halal. Ensure it is not derived from animal bones.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - natural mineral compound
explanation: Calcium carbonate is a natural mineral compound found in limestone, chalk, and marble. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - natural mineral compound
explanation: Silicon dioxide is a natural mineral used as an anti-caking agent. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - mineral-based white colorant
explanation: Titanium dioxide is a mineral-based white colorant. It is halal. Note: it has been banned in the EU since 2022 due to safety concerns, but this does not affect its halal status.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - derived from brown seaweed
explanation: Sodium alginate is extracted from brown seaweed and is halal. It is used as a thickener and gelling agent.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - derived from carob tree seeds
explanation: Locust bean gum is derived from the seeds of the carob tree and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - sap from acacia trees
explanation: Arabic gum is a natural gum from acacia trees, widely used in the food industry. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant fiber
explanation: Cellulose and its derivatives are derived from plant cell walls (wood pulp, cotton). They are halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - derived from chicory root
explanation: Inulin is a prebiotic fiber typically extracted from chicory root. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - natural sweetener from stevia plant leaves
explanation: Stevia is a natural zero-calorie sweetener extracted from the leaves of the Stevia rebaudiana plant. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - produced by fermentation of plant sugars; not an alcohol/intoxicant despite the name 'sugar alcohol'
explanation: Erythritol is a sugar alcohol produced by fermentation. Despite the name 'sugar alcohol,' it is not ethanol and is not intoxicating. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - derived from birch trees or corn cobs; not an intoxicant
explanation: Xylitol is a sugar alcohol derived from plant sources. It is not intoxicating and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - derived from glucose; not intoxicating
explanation: Sorbitol is a sugar alcohol produced from glucose, typically from corn starch. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - derived from starch (corn, tapioca, wheat)
explanation: Maltodextrin is a polysaccharide derived from starch, usually corn or tapioca. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - simple sugar derived from corn or other plant starch
explanation: Dextrose (glucose) is a simple sugar typically derived from corn starch. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - natural fruit sugar or produced from corn starch
explanation: Fructose is a natural sugar found in fruits and honey. High fructose corn syrup is produced enzymatically from corn starch. Both are halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - synthetic sweetener derived from sugar
explanation: Sucralose is a synthetic sweetener made by chlorinating sucrose. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - synthetic amino acid-based sweetener
explanation: Aspartame is a synthetic sweetener made from two amino acids (aspartic acid and phenylalanine). It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - synthetic sweetener
explanation: Acesulfame K is a synthetic sweetener and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - synthetic sweetener
explanation: Saccharin is a synthetic sweetener and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - synthetic preservative
explanation: Potassium sorbate is a synthetic preservative derived from sorbic acid. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - synthetic preservative used in bread
explanation: Calcium propionate is a synthetic mold inhibitor commonly used in bread. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - derived from vegetable oils
explanation: Tocopherols (vitamin E) are naturally derived from vegetable oils (soy, sunflower, palm). They are halal when plant-derived.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - produced synthetically or by microbial fermentation
explanation: Riboflavin (vitamin B2) is produced synthetically or by bacterial fermentation. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - synthetically produced vitamin
explanation: Niacin (vitamin B3) is synthetically produced and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - synthetically produced vitamin
explanation: Folic acid is a synthetic form of folate (vitamin B9). It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - mineral compound
explanation: Iron supplements and iron oxide colorants are mineral-based and halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - mineral compound
explanation: Zinc compounds used as supplements are mineral-based and halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - inorganic acid
explanation: Phosphoric acid is an inorganic acid commonly used in soft drinks. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - modified cellulose from plant fiber
explanation: CMC is a cellulose derivative used as a thickener. It is derived from plant fiber and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - derived from tara tree seeds
explanation: Tara gum is a natural thickener from the seeds of the tara tree. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - derived from konjac plant root
explanation: Konjac is a dietary fiber from the konjac plant root. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - blue-green algae
explanation: Spirulina is a blue-green algae used as a nutritional supplement and natural colorant. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived fiber from Plantago ovata seeds
explanation: Psyllium husk is a natural fiber from Plantago ovata plant seeds. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived seed
explanation: Flaxseed is a plant-derived seed rich in omega-3 fatty acids. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived seed
explanation: Chia seeds are plant-derived seeds and are halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived pseudo-cereal
explanation: Quinoa is a plant-derived pseudo-cereal and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - fungi are permissible; truffles mentioned in hadith
explanation: Mushrooms are halal. The Prophet (PBUH) mentioned truffles (a type of fungus) as a blessing. All edible mushrooms are permissible.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - single-celled fungi used in baking and fermentation
explanation: Yeast is a single-celled fungus used in bread-making and fermentation. It is halal. Brewer's yeast, despite its name, is halal when sold as a nutritional supplement (it does not contain alcohol).

ruling_hanafi: Halal - fish is halal; Hanafi school permits only fish (creatures with scales) from sea animals
ruling_shafii: Halal - all sea creatures are halal
ruling_maliki: Halal - all sea creatures are halal
ruling_hanbali: Halal - all sea creatures are halal
explanation: Fish is unanimously considered halal across all schools of Islamic jurisprudence. It does not require halal slaughter. The hadith states: 'Two dead animals and two types of blood have been made lawful: fish and locusts, liver and spleen.'

ruling_hanafi: Halal - dairy product from cow/goat milk
explanation: Butter from cow, goat, or sheep milk is halal. Some flavored butters may contain non-halal additives that should be checked.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - dairy product
explanation: Cream from cow, goat, or sheep milk is halal. Some cream products may contain gelatin or other non-halal stabilizers.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - fermented milk product using bacterial cultures
explanation: Plain yogurt is halal as it is made from milk fermented with bacterial cultures. Flavored yogurts may contain gelatin or other non-halal additives that should be checked.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - clarified butter from halal animals
explanation: Ghee (clarified butter) from cow, goat, or sheep milk is halal. It has been used in Islamic cuisine for centuries.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - marine plant
explanation: All types of edible seaweed are halal. They are marine plants/algae and are permissible.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - fermented soybean paste; trace alcohol from fermentation is negligible
explanation: Miso is a fermented soybean paste. While fermentation produces trace amounts of alcohol, the levels are negligible and it is not intoxicating. Most scholars consider it halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - fermented soybean product
explanation: Tempeh is a traditional fermented soybean product widely consumed in Southeast Asian Muslim communities. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived fruit
explanation: Tamarind is a tropical fruit and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - fat extracted from cacao beans
explanation: Cocoa butter is a natural fat extracted from cacao beans. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived fat from shea tree nuts
explanation: Shea butter is a fat extracted from the nuts of the shea tree. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - inorganic compound
explanation: Same as baking soda - an inorganic compound that is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - crystalline acid from grape juice; the compound itself is not wine and is not intoxicating
explanation: Cream of tartar is potassium bitartrate, which crystallizes from grape juice during winemaking. It is a distinct chemical compound that is not intoxicating and is considered halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - extracted from turmeric root
explanation: Turmeric oleoresin is a concentrated extract from turmeric root containing curcumin. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - red pigment from tomatoes
explanation: Lycopene is a natural red pigment extracted from tomatoes. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - extracted from marigold flowers
explanation: Lutein is a carotenoid pigment extracted from marigold flowers. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - naturally found in coffee, tea, and other plants; not considered an intoxicant by scholarly consensus
ruling_shafii: Halal - not an intoxicant by scholarly consensus
explanation: Caffeine is a natural stimulant found in coffee beans, tea leaves, and other plants. Despite early historical debate, the overwhelming scholarly consensus is that coffee and tea are halal. Excessive consumption that harms health would be makruh (disliked).

ruling_hanafi: Halal - commercially produced synthetically, not from animal sources
explanation: Despite its name deriving from 'taurus' (bull), commercial taurine is produced synthetically and is halal. It is not derived from animals in modern production.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - derived from soybeans
explanation: When lecithin is specifically labeled as 'soy lecithin,' it is derived from soybeans and is halal. Generic 'lecithin' without source specification is mushbooh.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - derived from sunflower seeds
explanation: Sunflower lecithin is derived from sunflower seeds and is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - produced by bacterial fermentation of sugars
explanation: Most commercial lactic acid is produced by bacterial fermentation of plant sugars. This form is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - synthetic/microbial preservative
explanation: Propionic acid is produced synthetically or by bacterial fermentation. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - synthetic organic acid
explanation: Fumaric acid is a food acid produced synthetically. It is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - produced by heating sugar/carbohydrates
explanation: Caramel color is produced by controlled heating of sugars (corn, wheat, or sugar). It is halal. The different types (E150a-d) vary in processing method but all use plant-derived sugars.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - natural pigment from fruits and vegetables (berries, grapes, red cabbage)
explanation: Anthocyanins are natural red-purple pigments found in berries, grapes, and red cabbage. They are halal.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - natural pigment from beetroot
explanation: Beetroot red is a natural colorant from beetroots. It is halal and a common halal alternative to carmine (E120).

ruling_hanafi: Halal - carbon from plant sources
explanation: Vegetable carbon is a black colorant made by charring plant material. When from plant sources (coconut, wood), it is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - pork-based cured meat
explanation: Traditional salami is typically made from pork. It is haram unless specifically made from halal-certified beef or turkey.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - pig intestine product
explanation: Sausage casings made from pig intestines are haram. Halal alternatives include collagen, cellulose, or casings from halal-slaughtered sheep.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - broth derived from pork bones and meat
explanation: Broth or stock made from pork bones and meat is haram. Tonkotsu ramen broth is a common example. Halal alternatives use chicken, beef, or vegetable bases.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - enzyme from pork pancreas
explanation: Trypsin derived from pork pancreas is haram. It is used in some food processing and pharmaceutical applications. Microbial or bovine alternatives exist.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - when confirmed from non-halal animal fat (pork or non-halal slaughtered animal)
explanation: When mono and diglycerides (E471) are confirmed to be derived from animal fat, particularly pork, they are haram. Plant-derived E471 (from soy or palm) is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - whey produced using non-halal animal rennet is contaminated
ruling_shafii: Haram - non-halal enzyme used in production
ruling_maliki: Haram - non-halal enzyme used in production
ruling_hanbali: Haram - non-halal enzyme used in production
explanation: Whey obtained as a byproduct of cheese made with non-halal animal rennet (especially pork pepsin) is considered haram. Whey from vegetable/microbial rennet cheese is halal.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - capsule shell made from pork gelatin
explanation: Many pharmaceutical and supplement capsules use pork gelatin for the shell. These are haram. Halal alternatives include HPMC (vegetarian capsules), fish gelatin capsules, or pullulan capsules.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - raw fat from non-halal slaughtered cattle
explanation: Suet is the hard fat around the kidneys of cattle and sheep. When from non-halal slaughtered animals, it is haram. Halal-certified suet is permissible.

ruling_hanafi: Haram - derived from animal bile, typically from non-halal slaughtered animals
explanation: Cholic acid is derived from animal bile, typically from cattle. Bile is considered impure (najis) by most scholars, and the animals are usually not halal-slaughtered.

ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - wool grease from sheep; the wool itself is halal but processing may introduce concerns
explanation: Lanolin is a wax secreted by sheep skin glands, extracted from wool. While sheep and their wool are halal, the industrial processing of lanolin may introduce non-halal substances. Used as a base for vitamin D3 production.

ruling_hanafi: Mushbooh - could be from halal (bovine/marine) or haram (pork) sources
explanation: Collagen without specified source could be bovine (potentially halal), marine (halal), or porcine (haram). Source verification is essential.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - gelatin derived from fish is permissible
explanation: Gelatin derived from fish skin and bones is halal in all schools. Fish does not require halal slaughter. Fish gelatin is a widely accepted halal alternative to pork/beef gelatin.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - plant-derived cellulose capsule
explanation: HPMC (hydroxypropyl methylcellulose) capsules are made from plant cellulose and are a halal alternative to gelatin capsules.

ruling_hanafi: Halal - enzyme produced by microbial fermentation
explanation: Microbial rennet is produced by fermentation using fungi or bacteria. It is halal and a widely accepted alternative to animal rennet in cheese-making.
